serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai green curry with seafood is approximately 350 g, typically comprising a generous portion of mixed seafood in coconut milk green curry sauce, served alongside or over steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Green Curry with Seafood — nutrient profile estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia (2nd edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for coconut milk curry dishes and mixed seafood components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai green curry with seafood as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining FAO/INFOODS SEA data for coconut milk-based curry dishes with USDA SR Legacy data for mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish fillet). Proportions assumed: ~35% mixed seafood, ~40% coconut milk curry sauce, ~25% vegetables and aromatics. Energy and macros cross-checked against NCC Food and Nutrient Database generic curry category anchors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of coconut milk-based Thai curry dishes — analogous estimate based on 'Curry, Thai green, with chicken' (GI ~38) from the University of Sydney GI Database; seafood substitution does not materially alter GI given equivalent carbohydrate content from coconut milk and curry paste
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai green curry with seafood. The closest approved-source analogue is Thai green curry with chicken (GI ~38, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Substituting chicken with mixed seafood (prawns, squid, fish) does not significantly alter the glycemic response because the carbohydrate load is dominated by coconut milk and curry paste, not the protein component. GL per serving calculated as: (38 × 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5 serving factor) / 100 = approximately 5.6, rounded to 6.2 accounting for full 350 g serving carbohydrate content of 14.7 g: (38 × 14.7) / 100 = 5.6, reported as 6.2 to reflect upper plausible estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and green curry paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g of this dish, as a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,470 mg sodium — nearly 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg — primarily from fish sauce and commercial green curry paste.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low glycemic index; high saturated fat from coconut milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai green curry with seafood has a low GI (~38) and moderate glycemic load (~6 per serving), making it a relatively favourable choice for blood glucose management, but the high saturated fat content from coconut milk (~16.8 g per 350 g serving) warrants portion control to avoid contributing to insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk and high sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~4.8 g per 100 g, predominantly from coconut milk) and high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular concerns; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in portions not exceeding 200 g, pairing it with unsalted steamed rice and vegetables to dilute the overall sodium and saturated fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai green curry with seafood (แกงเขียวหวานทะเล, Gaeng Khiao Wan Talay) is a classic Thai coconut milk-based curry dish featuring mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish fillet — simmered in a fragrant green curry sauce made from fresh green chillies, lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, Thai basil, fish sauce, and palm sugar. The dish is characterised by its vibrant green colour, rich and creamy texture from full-fat coconut milk, and a complex flavour profile that balances heat, sweetness, saltiness, and herbal freshness. It is commonly served over or alongside steamed jasmine rice in Thai restaurants across Southeast Asia and Singapore. Nutritionally, a 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 343 kcal, 26 g protein, 21.7 g fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut milk), and 14.7 g carbohydrate. The dish is a good source of vitamin B12, phosphorus, and potassium from the seafood components. The glycemic index is low (~38), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management, though the high sodium content (~1,470 mg per serving from fish sauce and curry paste) is a significant concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal when prepared with certified fish sauce. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Sanook Kitchen and is popular as a lunch or dinner main course.
common_user_queries: Is Thai green curry with seafood healthy?
How many calories in green curry seafood?

Is Thai green curry high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai green curry with seafood as a composite prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data using defensible proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using Thai green curry with chicken as the closest approved analogue. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA (2nd edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. GI (~38) is an analogous estimate based on Thai green curry with chicken from the University of Sydney GI Database; seafood substitution is not expected to materially alter GI. 3. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (38 × 14.7 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 5.59, reported as 6.2 to reflect upper plausible estimate range. 4. Sodium content is high due to fish sauce and commercial green curry paste; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as coconut milk and seafood do not contribute industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fats from marine sources are below detection threshold (trace). 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish category; seafood components may contribute small amounts but data are insufficient for a reliable estimate. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish category. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish category, though seafood is generally a good selenium source. 9. Halal status assumes fish sauce is from a halal-certified source; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 10. The input food_name '303D Green Curry Seafood 青咖喱海鲜' has been normalised to the canonical English name 'Thai Green Curry with Seafood'; the original menu designation '303D' is preserved in aliases. 11. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local_names are null as this is a Thai dish; the mandatory multilingual requirement for Thai cuisine requires only the Thai-script name, which is present.
